text: The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar at the umbilicus and three five millimeter trocars in the upper abdomen. The camera view is from the twelve millimeter trocar, showing entry into the abdomen with the five millimeter trocars.

The surgeon grasps the fundus of the gallbladder and retracts it upward toward the head, revealing the liver, gallbladder, stomach, and duodenum. Adhesions between the omentum, mesentery, and liver are being dissected.
The surgeon opens the anterior and posterior peritoneal reflections at the infundibulum of the gallbladder, exposing critical structures. The dissection is extended onto the liver bed on both sides.

The surgeon carefully dissects the cystic artery high on the body of the gallbladder, maintaining a safe distance from the portal structures to prevent injury to the common bile duct or hepatic artery.

The surgeon clears the inferior edge of the gallbladder, isolating the cystic duct and cystic artery. A Maryland dissector is used to dissect behind the cystic artery and further clear the area behind the cystic duct at its junction with the gallbladder infundibulum.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety by dissecting further up onto the cystic plate where the gallbladder meets the liver bed. The cystic duct and cystic artery are visible, along with the cleared inferior edge of the gallbladder up to the cystic plate, showing two structures entering the gallbladder.
The surgeon exposes the common bile duct while maintaining the critical view of safety, which helps to minimize the risk of injury to the common bile duct or hepatic artery.

The surgeon identifies a stone within the cystic duct and places a single clip laterally on the duct. The cystic duct is partially opened, and a Maryland dissector is used to extract the stone from the duct and subsequently from the abdomen.

Three clips are placed medially on the cystic duct, followed by two clips medially and one laterally on the cystic artery. The nodular structure visible on the artery is identified as the node of Collot. The cystic duct and artery are then divided.
The surgeon ensures complete hemostasis and uses electrocautery to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder is then placed into an endocatch bag for removal.
The surgeon verifies complete hemostasis in the operative field and irrigates the area as needed, ensuring a clear and clean surgical site.
